text: Single port laparoscopic heartman reversal. This is the case of a fifty nine year old man who underwent a laparotomic heartmans procedure because of peritonitis due to a complicated diverticular disease in another hospital. Nine months after this procedure a laparoscopic heartman reversal using the single port technique was planned in our institution.

The surgeon excises the colostomy, detaches it from the abdominal wall, and introduces the anvil of the circular stapler into the colon, securing it with a hand-sewn pursing suture. The colon is repositioned into the peritoneal cavity. The colostomy site serves as an access point for the single port device, with one trocar for the camera and two working trocars, achieving pneumoperitoneum at twelve millimeters of mercury. Standard laparoscopic instruments and an intracorporeal articulating sealing device are used. The surgeon, positioned between the patient's legs, performs adhesiolysis and mobilizes the descending colon and splenic flexure from lateral to medial, ensuring sufficient mobility of the left colon.
The surgeon, assisted from the right side of the patient, detaches loops of the small bowel located in the pelvis to uncage the rectal sigmoidal stump. The stump is then mobilized and isolated.

The surgeon advances the circular stapler into the rectosigmoidal stump and connects it with the anvil to create a tension-free anastomosis. The anastomosis is tested with saline and air for integrity. Absorbable sutures are used to close the ostomy site, and the skin is closed with a pursed ring suture using non-absorbable material.
